coredata.dc:title: Probing the fluorescence quenching mechanism of N-doped carbon quantum dots by inorganic ions
coredata.dc:creator: Dong, Xipeng
Qi, Hongxia
Zhai, Zizhuo
Li, Wanqing
Zhang, Pudun
coredata.dc:description: Fluorescence quenching is a common phenomenon of carbon quantum dots (CQDs) and has been utilized to detect various compounds. Since CQDs are prepared by varied synthesis routes and by diverse raw materials, a variety of fluorescence quenching mechanisms have been proposed. In this work, blue-emitted nitrogen-doped CQDs (N-CQDs) were prepared using citric acid and ethylenediamine as precursors, and its fluorescence quenching by inorganic ions was probed. It is found that only oxidizing inorganic ions (including Fe3+, MnO4 −, Cr2O7 2−, CrO4 2−, ClO−, BrO3 −, BrO3 −+Br− and S2O8 2−) can remarkably quench the fluorescence, and a redox quenching mechanism was further proposed by analyzing the UV–vis absorption spectra, the fluorescence lifetime spectra, the electron paramagnetic resonance spectra, the zeta potential and the X-ray photoelectron spectra. In addition, when overlap between the absorption spectra of ions and the excitation or emission spectra of N-CQDs occurs, inner filter effect (IFE) can also quench the fluorescence as an auxiliary way, although it is less important compared with redox quenching. This work contributes to a better understanding of CQDs in sensing field.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs)
Fluorescence quenching
Inorganic ions
Redox quenching
Inner filter effect (IFE)

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fluorescence quenching measurement of the N-CQDs
Morphological, structural and optical properties of N-CQDs
Fluorescence quenching of N-CQDs by inorganic ions
Fluorescence quenching mechanism of N-CQDs with KMnO4
Fluorescence quenching mechanism of N-CQDs with other oxidizing inorganic ions

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors are grateful to Mrs. Yu Kang and Mrs. Yingchun Yu for fluorescence lifetime and TEM measurements, respectively.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM image of N-CQDs and its size distribution histogram (the inset).

(a) FTIR spectrum, (b) 1H NMR spectrum, (c) 13C NMR spectrum, and (d) UV–vis absorption spectrum, fluorescence excitation and emission spectra of the N-CQDs. The inset of (d) shows the photographs of the N-CQDs aqueous solution under daylight (left) and UV light (right, 365 nm).

(a) and (b) are fluorescence quenching efficiency of the aqueous N-CQDs (2.0 μg mL−1) in the presence of varied cations and anions, as well as their cationic and anionic mixtures, respectively. (c) Fluorescence quenching efficiency of the solutions of pure N-CQDs, KBrO3 + N-CQDs and K2S2O8 + N-CQDs at different conditions. (d) Relationship between the quenching efficiency of the mixture KBrO3 + KBr and the incubation time at pH = 2.

(a) and (c) display plots of fluorescence quenching efficiencies of the aqueous N-CQDs (2.0 μg mL−1) versus MnO4 − concentrations (0–300 μM) at pH = 7 and pH = 2, respectively. The insets show their corresponding fluorescence spectra. (b) and (d) show the UV–vis absorption spectra of N-CQDs solution with various concentrations of MnO4 − (0–300 μM) and pure KMnO4 solution (300 μM) at pH = 7 and pH = 2, respectively. The inset in (b) shows absorption spectra of N-CQDs solution, KMnO4 solution, and their mixtures.
(a) Fluorescence lifetime of N-CQDs before and after adding KMnO4. (b) XPS high-resolution spectrum of Mn2p. (c) UV–vis absorption spectra of KMnO4 and the excitation and emission spectra of N-CQDs.

(a) Fluorescence lifetime decay curves of N-CQDs before and after adding Fe3+, Cr2O7 2−, CrO4 2− and ClO− at pH = 7. (b) Fluorescence lifetime decay curves of N-CQDs before and after adding BrO3 −, BrO3 −+Br− and S2O8 2− at pH = 2. (c) to (i) XPS high-resolution spectra of Fe2p, Cr2p, Cl2p, Br3d and S2p of mixtures of N-CQDs and varied ions.
UV–vis absorption spectra of different ions, and excitation and emission spectra of N-CQDs.
Schematic diagram for preparing N-CQDs and their quenching by oxidizing inorganic ions.
Schematic redox quenching mechanism of N-CQDs with oxidizing inorganic ions.
Contribution of IFE of KMnO4 to the fluorescence quenching of N-CQDs.a)

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: a) The measurement results are the average of three experiments.
b) Fcor, 0 is the corrected fluorescence intensity of N-CQDs in the absence of KMnO4, taken as 5982.2.

head.ce:title: Probing the fluorescence quenching mechanism of N-doped carbon quantum dots by inorganic ions

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry, Beijing University of Chemical Technology, Beijing 100029, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemistry, Beijing University of Chemical Technology, Beijing 100029, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Analysis and Test Center, Beijing University of Chemical Technology, Beijing 100029, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Analysis and Test Center, Beijing University of Chemical Technology, Beijing 100029, China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: No. 15 Beisanhuan East Road, Chaoyang, Beijing 100029, China.

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: X.P. Dong and H.X. Qi contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Fluorescence quenching is a common phenomenon of carbon quantum dots (CQDs) and has been utilized to detect various compounds. Since CQDs are prepared by varied synthesis routes and by diverse raw materials, a variety of fluorescence quenching mechanisms have been proposed. In this work, blue-emitted nitrogen-doped CQDs (N-CQDs) were prepared using citric acid and ethylenediamine as precursors, and its fluorescence quenching by inorganic ions was probed. It is found that only oxidizing inorganic ions (including Fe3+, MnO4 −, Cr2O7 2−, CrO4 2−, ClO−, BrO3 −, BrO3 −+Br− and S2O8 2−) can remarkably quench the fluorescence, and a redox quenching mechanism was further proposed by analyzing the UV–vis absorption spectra, the fluorescence lifetime spectra, the electron paramagnetic resonance spectra, the zeta potential and the X-ray photoelectron spectra. In addition, when overlap between the absorption spectra of ions and the excitation or emission spectra of N-CQDs occurs, inner filter effect (IFE) can also quench the fluorescence as an auxiliary way, although it is less important compared with redox quenching. This work contributes to a better understanding of CQDs in sensing field.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have attracted considerable attention due to its tunable fluorescence, excellent photostability, solubility and biocompatibility, as well as low-cost and easy preparation [1–5]. The fluorescence quantum yield of CQDs can be increased by altering carbon framework through heteroatom doping, in which nitrogen doping is one of the most effective and available strategies owing to the existence of five valence electrons on nitrogen atom and the comparable size to carbon atom [6–8]. In view of these advantages, much efforts have been devoted to apply CQDs in various fields, such as sensing [9–15], photo-electrocatalysis [16,17], drug delivery [18], bioimaging [19–21], light emitting diodes (LED) [22–24], photothermal conversion [25,26], and information safety [27]. As one of the most important properties of CQDs, fluorescence plays a major role in many applications. However, a big challenge for CQDs is that the fluorescence can be quenched.

The quenching mechanism of a fluorescence molecule generally includes dynamic quenching effect (DQE) and static quenching effect (SQE). DQE is a process that the excited CQDs return to ground state via electron transfer or energy transfer when excited CQDs collide with quenchers. It is characterized by the decline of fluorescence lifetime and the consistence of absorption spectrum [28–30]. On the other hand, SQE is a process that new ground-state non-fluorescent complexes are formed between CQDs and quenchers. Different from DQE, the UV absorption spectrum will change when adding quenchers. Moreover, the fluorescence lifetime of CQDs remains nearly constant [31–33]. Photoinduced electron transfer (PET) and fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) are two common DQEs for interpreting the fluorescence quenching. PET is an electron transfer process between CQDs and quenchers, in which free radicals are produced by exciting electron receptors or donors [34,35]. FRET is a non-radiative energy transfer process through intermolecular long-range dipole–dipole coupling between CQDs and quenchers within 10 nm, and the absorption spectrum of quencher must overlap with the excitation or emission spectrum of CQDs as much as possible [11,36–38]. Except for DQE and SQE, inner filter effect (IFE) is also used to interpret quenching. However, IFE is essentially a selective filtering process of light when the absorption spectrum of quencher overlaps with the excitation or emission spectrum of CQDs. Simply put, IFE is a process that the emitted light is re-absorbed by quencher [39–42]. The essential difference between IFE and FRET is that the fluorescence lifetime of the former remains unchanged while the latter changes.

Although the fluorescence quenching mechanism of CQDs had been explored by a few researchers [43–45], it still has a long way to go because the CQDs prepared by varied synthesis routes and by diverse raw materials may lead to a variety of fluorescence quenching mechanism. In our previous work [46], the blue-emitted nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs) with high fluorescence quantum yield (44 %) were prepared via low-cost hydrothermal route using citric acid (CA) and ethylenediamine (EDA) as precursors. A platform for sensitively detecting ClO− was further developed via quenching of the CQDs. When optimizing its selectivity and sensitivity, an interesting phenomenon caught our attention that when different inorganic ions were added to the N-CQDs solution, they showed markedly different fluorescence quenching abilities. To further probe the fluorescence quenching mechanism of the N-CQDs by inorganic ions, we prepared the N-CQDs with blue emission and investigated the effects of various inorganic ions on the fluorescence intensity of N-CQDs. It is found that the oxidizing inorganic ions can efficiently quench the fluorescence of the N-CQDs, and therefore a redox quenching mechanism was presented and validated. Furthermore, we also found that IFE is a secondary contribution to the total fluorescence quenching of N-CQDs for those oxidizing inorganic ions with absorption spectra that overlap with the excitation or emission spectra of N-CQDs. A brief overview is displayed in Scheme 1 .

CA, EDA, SnCl4·2H2O, FeCl2, FeCl3, AlCl3·6H2O, CuSO4·5H2O, AgNO3, NaCl, KCl, MgCl2, CaCl2, CrCl3·6H2O, ZnCl2, NH4Cl, NiCl2·6H2O, MnSO4·H2O, Hg (NO3)2, K2CrO4, K2Cr2O7, KMnO4, KBrO3, KBr, K2S2O8, KH2PO4, Na2SO3, NaHCO3, Na2CO3, and NaB4O7·10H2O were analytical reagents (A.R.). All the anion and cation solutions used in the experiments were prepared by their salt compounds.

Based on our previous work [46], N-CQDs were prepared from CA and EDA via a hydrothermal route at 200 °C for 5 h, and then were purified further by a home-made Sephadex G-15 gel column, which was prepared following a procedure described in the Supplementary materials of the publication [46]. The obtained N-CQDs powders were always stored in a desiccator at room temperature.

Transmission electron microscopic (TEM) image was acquired on a Hitachi-7700 (Hitachi, Japan) at 200 KV. X-ray diffractive (XRD) pattern was obtained on an Ultima IV X-ray diffraction system (Rigaku, Japan) with a Cu Kα radiation (λ = 0.15418 nm) by scanning from 5° to 90° at an angle interval of 0.02°. Raman spectrum was acquired by an inVia Reflex Raman spectrometer (Renishaw, UK) with an excitation wavelength of 514 nm laser. Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectrum was obtained on a Nexus 8700 FTIR spectrometer (Thermo Electron, Madison, WI, USA) with a deuterated triglycine sulfate (DTGS) detector. Both the 1H and 13C nuclear magnetic resonance (NMR) spectra were collected on a Bruker AVANCE NEO 600 MHz instrument (Bruker, Germany). The ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) absorption spectra were gathered on a UV-3600 spectrometer (Shimadzu, Japan). The fluorescence spectra were recorded on a F-7000 fluorescence spectrometer (Hitachi, Japan) with slit width of 5 nm for both excitation and emission at PMT voltage of 600 V. The fluorescence decay curves were measured on a FLS 980 steady-state/transient fluorescence spectrometer (Edinburgh, UK). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) characterizations were performed on an ESCALAB 250 XPS instrument (Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA) with a monochromatic Al Kα radiation source (150 W) and a spot size of 500 μm. Electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR) spectra were collected on a E500-10/12 Bruker spectrometer (Bruker, Germany). The ζ-potentials were measured at 25 °C using a Zetasizer Nano ZS90 instrument (Malvern Instrument Co., UK).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence quenching measurement of the N-CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The as-prepared N-CQDs were firstly diluted with deionized water to obtain a solution of 2.0 μg mL−1. Varied anion and cation solutions (Sn4+, Fe2+, Fe3+, Al3+, Cu2+, Hg2+, Ag+, Cr3+, Zn2+, Ni2+, Mn2+, Cr2O7 2−, CrO4 2−, MnO4 −, ClO−, BrO3 −, Br−, S2O8 2−, HPO4 2−, CO3 2−, B2O7 2−, SO3 2−) with concentration of 3 mM and a mixed solution of BrO3 − and Br− were obtained by dissolving their respective salts in deionized water. Subsequently, 3 mL of different anion and cation solutions were added into the N-CQDs solutions with equal volume, respectively, and their fluorescence emission spectra were recorded with an excitation wavelength of 342 nm after incubation of 30 s at room temperature (25 °C).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Morphological, structural and optical properties of N-CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The morphology of the N-CQDs was determined by TEM. The nearly monodisperse quasi-spherical particles with an average diameter of 2.6 ± 0.2 nm are demonstrated (Fig. 1 ). However, the well-resolved lattice fringes of graphite were not found by high resolution TEM due to the amorphous structure of the prepared N-CQDs, which can be confirmed by XRD and Raman spectroscopy. The XRD pattern (Fig. S1) shows a very broad and weak peak at about 23◦, attributing to the diffraction of (002) crystal plane of carbon-based materials. However, the diffraction peak is dispersed by the very small size of N-CQDs and becomes very broad, suggesting that the prepared N-CQDs are indeed amorphous. On the other hand, The Raman spectrum (Fig. S2) displays evident D and G bands (1292 cm−1 and 1561 cm−1), which are respectively caused by the amorphous and crystalline states [47,48]. The intensity of D band is much stronger than the one of G band (ID/IG = 1.49), suggesting that the amorphous state is dominant in the N-CQDs. Our result is very similar to the ones reported in literatures [49,50].

The FTIR spectrum (Fig. 2 a) shows the existence of hydroxyl (v O-H 3417 cm−1), amino (v N-H 3243 cm−1, δ N-H 1388 cm−1, and δ C-N 1218 cm−1), aromatic ring (v (ph)C-H 3077 cm−1, v (ph)C=C 1481 cm−1, and γ ph 779 cm−1), aliphatic C-H (v (al)C-H 2969 cm−1), carboxyl (v C=O, carboxyl dimer at 1695 cm−1 and free carboxyl at1768 cm−1) and amide (amide I 1657 cm−1 and amide II 1553 cm−1) groups on the prepared N-CQDs. That the intensity of the band at 1553 cm−1 is larger than the one at 1657 cm−1 indicates the existence of pyridine structure (v C=N of pyridine, at 1553 cm−1) [46]. Besides, NMR spectroscopy is also applied to validate the structure of the prepared N-CQDs. The chemical shifts at 2.8–4.1 ppm (the CH2 connected to N and O), 5.6–6.0 ppm (the CH2 connected to two aromatic rings), 7.1–7.6 ppm (aromatic C-H) and 8.5–8.8 ppm (aromatic amide N-H) in 1H NMR spectrum (Fig. 2b), and the ones at 40–45 ppm (the sp3 hybridized carbon of aliphatic group), 79–82 ppm (the sp3 hybridized carbon connected to N and O), 100–110 ppm (aromatic sp2 carbon), 141–168 ppm (the amide sp2 carbon) and 170–179 ppm (the carboxyl sp2 carbon) in 13C NMR spectrum (Fig. 2c) reveal similar results to FTIR. Both FTIR and NMR suggest that there are abundant amino, amide, hydroxyl and carboxyl groups on N-CQDs, which endow the N-CQDs excellent water solubility and prominent photoluminescent property.

The optical spectra of aqueous N-CQDs are displayed in Fig. 2d. The solution appears light brown under daylight and bright blue under UV light (365 nm) (the inset of Fig. 2d). Two absorption peaks at 245 nm and 345 nm are observed, attributing to π → π* transition of aromatic C = C bonds and n → π* transition of C = O bonds, respectively. The fluorescence spectra display the optimal excitation and emission wavelengths at 342 nm and 440 nm. Moreover, the fluorescence intensity of the N-CQDs maintains almost unchangeable in high ionic strength (0.0–3.0 M of NaCl), ultralong storage time (0–6 months) and broad pH range (5–11) [46], suggesting that it has good tolerance to circumstance.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence quenching of N-CQDs by inorganic ions
ce:section.ce:para: The influences of a variety of inorganic ions on the fluorescence intensity of N-CQDs were investigated. The fluorescence quenching efficiency is calculated by (F0-F)/F0, where F0 and F represent the fluorescence intensity in the absence and presence of inorganic ions, respectively. Fig. 3 a and 3b summarize the quenching results by incubating various cations and anions with N-CQDs for 30 s at 25 °C. It indicates from Fig. 3a that Fe3+ has the most remarkable quenching effect among the cations and the quenching efficiency is nearly 100 %. In addition, Sn4+ also exhibits a distinct quenching effect and the quenching efficiency exceeds 50 %. On the other hand, the anions Cr2O7 2−, CrO4 2−, MnO4 − and ClO− show almost complete quenching for N-CQDs (Fig. 3b). Moreover, both the cationic and anionic mixtures also exhibit a nearly complete quenching efficiency, suggesting that no mutual suppression effect exists in the cations or anions. It is worth noting that both cations and anions that can quench the fluorescence of N-CQDs are oxidizing. Hence, it is reasonable to infer that the redox reactions between the inorganic oxidants and N-CQDs is the dominant factor of quenching. However, we also noticed that BrO3 − and S2O8 2− have no quenching effect, although they have strong oxidative ability, which is in contradiction with the presumption.

Considering that most of anions’ redox reactions require H+ to participate in [51], all the anion solutions were adjusted to pH = 2 using 0.05 M H2SO4 solution and were then mixed with N-CQDs for 30 s. The results (Fig. 3b) show that only these four anions (Cr2O7 2−, CrO4 2−, MnO4 − and ClO−) have excellent quenching effect, while others still do not. However, it is found that the fluorescence of the two acidic mixtures of BrO3 − and S2O8 2− with N-CQDs almost disappear after 48 h of incubation (Fig. 3c), suggesting that both of these two strong oxidants also have quenching ability. That they did not work in 30 s is due to their very slow oxidation rates at room temperature. Generally, the reaction rate can be improved by raising temperature, we thus further explored the effect of temperature on quenching. It clearly shows from Fig. 3c that S2O8 2− can completely quench the fluorescence within 10 min of incubation at 60 °C, owing to the generation of numerous SO4
− radicals at higher temperature, which display the extra-strong oxidizing activity and therefore contribute to accelerate the oxidation quenching. The fluorescence intensity of pure N-CQDs solution also decreases by nearly 25 % when the temperature is raised to 60 °C, which attributes to the accelerated collisions of fluorescent molecules at higher temperature. In addition, it is noticed that BrO3 − has a quenching efficiency of only about 44 % within 10 min of incubation at 60 °C, suggesting that the reaction rate of BrO3 − is slower than S2O8 2− at 60 °C. In fact, BrO3 − hardly exhibits the oxidation property due to the very slow rate. In order to accelerate the oxidation rate, bromate is always used together with bromide, i.e., BrO3 − + Br−, which shows a significant oxidizing ability at acidic condition [52]. In this condition, Bromine radical (Br·) with a high oxidizing activity is generated. Our results demonstrate that the quenching efficiency of the acidic mixture of BrO3 − + Br− becomes remarkable after 10 min of incubation and reaches the largest value (>90 %) in 20 min at room temperature (Fig. 3d). Based on the above results, a conclusion that the fluorescence of N-CQDs is quenched by inorganic ions with oxidation ability can be drawn.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence quenching mechanism of N-CQDs with KMnO4
ce:section.ce:para: To demonstrate the redox quenching mechanism of oxidizing inorganic ions on N-CQDs, KMnO4 was first selected as the quencher for in-depth research based on its naked-eye color change and the variable reduction products at different acidity. First, the N-CQDs solutions of 2.0 μg mL−1 were mixed with a series of KMnO4 solutions (0–300 μM) in equal volume under neutral condition (pH = 7), and the fluorescence spectra as well as the intensity at 440 nm were recorded after 30 s (Fig. 4 a). As expected, the fluorescence quenching efficiency rapidly increases with increasing the concentration of MnO4 −, and the quenching is nearly finished when the concentration exceeds 30 μM. Since the reduction product of KMnO4 is MnO2 under neutral condition, the UV–vis spectra of the above mixtures and the pure KMnO4 solution were further collected. Compared with N-CQDs and KMnO4 solutions, the spectra of mixture solutions (Fig. 4b) are obviously raised in the range of 300–600 nm, conforming well to the absorption spectrum of MnO2 sol, which has a broad absorption spectrum in the range of 210–600 nm [53,54]. The inset of Fig. 4b shows the comparison of the spectra of N-CQDs, KMnO4 solution and their mixtures. The evident rise in the range 300–600 nm demonstrates the presence of MnO2. These results suggest that the redox reaction indeed occurred at pH = 7 between the N-CQDs and KMnO4.

Correspondingly, the reaction between KMnO4 and the N-CQDs under acidic condition (pH = 2) was also conducted in order to confirm the occurrence of the redox reaction more convincingly. The experimental conditions were the same as the ones at pH = 7 except that additional 0.3 mL of 0.05 M H2SO4 was added into N-CQDs solution (2.0 μg mL−1) to adjust pH to 2. Similarly, the fluorescence spectra and the quenching efficiencies at varied concentrations of MnO4 − are also depicted in Fig. 4c, and a 100 % quenching is obtained when the concentration of KMnO4 reaches 30 μM. Noting that the quenching efficiency slightly decreases in the range 0–10 μM, this is reasonable because MnO4 − was reduced to Mn2+ in acidic condition, which has a slight fluorescence enhancement, as shown in Fig. 3a. In addition, the UV–vis spectra of the mixture solutions are not raised in the range of 300–600 nm (Fig. 4d), further indicating that no MnO2 is formed. Since redox reaction involves the electron transfer and conforms to the dynamic quenching mechanism, the fluorescence lifetime spectra (Fig. 5 a) of the pure N-CQDs and its mixture with 3 mM KMnO4 are therefore collected. For N-CQDs the average lifetime of 12.66 ns is obtained by fitting the time-resolved decay data to the double-exponential function with a fast component of τ1 = 6.43 ns and a slow component of τ2 = 15.02 ns. After adding 3 mM KMnO4, the lifetime decreases to 10.56 ns, matching the dynamic quenching mechanism perfectly. To further validate the redox quenching mechanism, the XPS survey scan (Fig. S2a) of the dried mixture of N-CQDs with KMnO4 at pH = 7 was also collected. Fig. 5b shows that the high-resolution spectrum of Mn2p involves three peaks, i.e., Mn2p3/2 at 643.0 eV, Mn2p1/2 at 654.7 eV of MnO2 and the residual Mn (VII) at 646.6 eV, which further confirms the occurrence of redox reaction between N-CQDs and KMnO4. These results definitely demonstrate that KMnO4 can efficiently quench the fluorescence of N-CQDs via a redox reaction, regardless of whether the solution is acidic or neutral.

Moreover, we also noticed that there are significant overlaps between the absorption spectrum of KMnO4 and excitation and emission spectra of N-CQDs in the ranges of 280–400 nm and 450–550 nm (Fig. 5c). As a result, the fluorescence intensity of the N-CQDs may also be reduced by the IFE of KMnO4. In order to explore the contribution of IFE to the fluorescence quenching, the fluorescence intensities of N-CQDs solutions with varied concentrations of MnO4 − were corrected using Eq. (1) [54]
(1)

where CF is the correction factor, Fobsd the observed fluorescence intensity and Fcor the corrected fluorescence intensity after removing IFE, Aex and Aem the absorbance at the wavelength of optimal excitation (342 nm) and emission (440 nm), respectively, g the distance between the edge of the excitation beam and the quartz cuvette, d the width of the quartz cuvette, and s the thickness of the excitation beam. In this work, g, d and s are 0.40 cm, 1.00 cm and 0.10 cm, respectively.

The percentage of IFE accounting for the total fluorescence quenching is quantitatively calculated using Eq. (2),
(2)
IFE % =
F
cor
-
F
obsd
F
cor , 0

F
obsd
× 100
where Fcor,0 represents the corrected fluorescence intensity of N-CQDs solution without KMnO4. For the N-CQDs solution of 2.0 μg mL−1, a series of KMnO4 solutions with varied concentrations were added, and the contributions of IFE were calculated and summarized in Table 1 . It indicates that the maximum percentage of IFE in the total quenching effect of N-CQDs is no more than 22.2 % under neutral condition. Our results suggest that the fluorescence quenching of N-CQDs by KMnO4 is dominated by redox reaction and IFE, and the former plays a vital role in this process.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence quenching mechanism of N-CQDs with other oxidizing inorganic ions
ce:section.ce:para: To verify that the obtained redox quenching mechanism is also applicable to others, the oxidizing ions (Fe3+, Cr2O7 2−, CrO4 2−, ClO−, BrO3 −, BrO3 −+Br− and S2O8 2−) with strong quenching efficiency were further investigated. First, the fluorescence lifetime decay curves of N-CQDs with these ions at different acidity are collected (Fig. 6 a and b). All the mixtures of N-CQDs and various ions exhibit remarkably shortened fluorescence lifetimes compared with pure N-CQDs under same acidity, which confirm the DQE mechanism. The specific lifetime data are given in Table S1.

The XPS survey scans of the N-CQDs with these ions were also achieved, as shown in Fig. S3b to S3h. Fig. 6c–i show the high-resolution XPS spectra of Fe2p, Cr2p, Cl2p, Br3d and S2p. The appearance of peaks of Fe2+ at 710.4 eV and 724.0 eV (Fig. 6c), Cr3+ at 576.6 eV and 586.4 eV (Fig. 6d and e), Cl− at 198.5 eV and 200.1 eV (Fig. 6f), Br− at 69.4 eV (Fig. 6g), Br− at 69.4 eV and at 70.4 eV (Fig. 6h), and SO4 2− at 168.4 eV and 169.5 eV (Fig. 6i), strongly indicate the formation of their respective reduction products. Both fluorescence lifetime and XPS results firmly demonstrate the redox quenching mechanism of N-CQDs by oxidizing inorganic ions.

Furthermore, the redox reactions between N-CQDs and these oxidizing inorganic ions were also verified via EPR by investigating the ground-state properties of luminescent species in the N-CQDs. As shown in Fig S4, the EPR signal of N-CQDs at g = 1.9330 reveals singly occupied orbital in ground-state N-CQDs, which indicates that the N-CQDs could act as electron donors or acceptors during reaction. The decreases of the g values of N-CQDs after quenching by oxidizing ions indicate the occurrence of electron transfer from the N-CQDs to oxidizing ions [55]. In addition, the ζ-potentials of N-CQDs with these ions were also measured to verify the redox fluorescence quenching mechanism. As shown in Table S2, the ζ-potential of N-CQDs was −6.053 mV, which was caused by the presence of –OH and –COOH functional groups on the surface of N-CQDs. With the addition of oxidizing ions, the ζ-potentials become less negative, suggesting that the electron transfer from N-CQDs to ions quenchers indeed occur. Based on the above discussions, a schematic redox fluorescence quenching mechanism for N-CQDs with oxidizing inorganic ions (Scheme 2 ) is presented.

In addition, the UV–vis absorption spectra of the oxidizing ions were also recorded and compared with the fluorescence spectra of N-CQDs in order to investigate their respective IFEs. Fig. 7 a–c show significant overlaps between the absorption spectra and the excitation and/or emission spectra of N-CQDs, suggesting that there exists the IFE for Fe3+, Cr2O7 2− and CrO4 2−. The detailed IFE data of Fe3+, Cr2O7 2− and CrO4 2− are given in Tables S3–S5. It reveals that both Cr2O7 2− and CrO4 2− have a relatively obvious IFE. Nevertheless, the maximum contributions of IFE to the total quenching do not exceed 33.22 % or 28.90 %, respectively. On the other hand, the contribution of IFE to the total quenching is just less than 8.91 % for Fe3+. The others without obvious overlaps (Fig. 7d–g) have no IFE. The fluorescence lifetime, the EPR spectra, the zeta potential, the XPS and the absorption results confirm that the redox fluorescence quenching of N-CQDs is universal and predominant for oxidizing ions. In addition, IFE is another factor when their optical spectra are overlapped.

The fluorescence quenching of N-CQDs by inorganic ions was systematically investigated using fluorescence lifetime spectroscopy, EPR, Zeta potential, XPS, UV–vis absorption, and fluorescence spectroscopy. A redox quenching mechanism was proposed based on the shortened lifetime and the reduced products when oxidizing ions were added into N-CQDs solution. In addition, an additional IFE quenching was also found for the ions with absorption spectra overlapped with the excitation or emission spectra of N-CQDs. Compared with redox quenching, however, the IFE has a minor contribution to total quenching. This work is fundamental for studying the fluorescence quenching of N-CQDs and will promote the exploration of carbon-based fluorescence materials in sensing field.

Xipeng Dong: Methodology, Validation, Writing – original draft. Hongxia Qi: Data curation, Methodology, Writing – original draft. Zizhuo Zhai: Data curation, Formal analysis. Wanqing Li: Formal analysis, Investigation. Pudun Zhang: Conceptualization, Investigation, Methodology, Supervision, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors are grateful to Mrs. Yu Kang and Mrs. Yingchun Yu for fluorescence lifetime and TEM measurements, respectively.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.microc.2023.109854.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
